elements.sent-2411-001: Reliable wind speed data is crucial for applications such as estimating local (future) wind power.
elements.sent-2411-002: Global Climate Models (GCMs) and Regional Climate Models (RCMs) provide forecasts over multi-decadal periods.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, their outputs vary substantially, and higher-resolution models come with increased computational demands.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this study, we analyze how the spatial resolution of different GCMs and RCMs affects the reliability of simulated wind speeds and wind power, using ERA5 data as a reference.
elements.sent-2411-005: We present a systematic procedure for model evaluation for wind resource assessment as a downstream task.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our results show that higher-resolution GCMs and RCMs do not necessarily preserve wind speeds more accurately.
elements.sent-2411-007: Instead, the choice of model, both for GCMs and RCMs, is more important than the resolution or GCM boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-008: The IPSL model preserves the wind speed distribution particularly well in Europe, producing the most accurate wind power forecasts relative to ERA5 data.
elements.sent-2411-009: Wind is expected to become a dominant source of energy in future power supply \citep{iea .}
elements.sent-2411-010: For wind to be a reliable source of energy, accurate regional wind forecasts over multi-decadal periods are necessary.
elements.sent-2411-011: Climate model output is a natural choice of data for such analyses, as they provide projections on multi-decadal timescales \citep[e.g.][]{pryor2020climate, ndiaye2022projected .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Climate models are complex physical earth system models whose outputs can differ substantially with large inherent model uncertainties \citep{zhang2024quantify .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Given this variability, it is crucial to assess the skill of climate models, especially their ability to perform in specific applications of interest such as wind power forecasting \citep{moemken2016decadal, isphording2024standardized .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Previous research revealed that a higher spatial wind data resolution is more desirable.
elements.sent-2411-015: Not only because it provides more local forecasts \cite{rummukainen2016added , but because the resolution at which a model operates affects which weather phenomena it can resolve \citep{lucas2021convection .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: While higher-resolution models are able to resolve more fine-grained weather phenomena, they come with computational challenges.
elements.sent-2411-017: All models --no matter the spatial resolution-- require modeling assumptions and therefore contain errors and uncertainties.
elements.sent-2411-018: It remains unclear which climate data, at which spatial resolution, best represents future wind speeds.
elements.sent-2411-019: Addressing this question requires a careful evaluation of the different climate model data available.
elements.sent-2411-020: CMIP6 represents the most recent effort to organize and standardize global climate models (GCMs) \citep{eyring2016overview and making the resulting multi-model output publicly available.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Compared to its predecessor CMIP5, CMIP6 offers significant improvements in model performance \citep{carvalho2021wind , particularly regarding surface wind speed simulations \citep{miao2023evaluation .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: A key limitation of GCMs remains their low spatial resolution, typically at $\geq 100 \text{ km}$.
elements.sent-2411-023: There are techniques to increase the spatial resolution of global climate model output.
elements.sent-2411-024: Regional climate models use GCMs as their input and run at finer resolution \citep{vautard2021evaluation to resolve more fine-grained weather phenomena physically.}
elements.sent-2411-025: This process, called dynamical downscaling, is, however, computationally intensive.
elements.sent-2411-026: Statistical downscaling methods offer a less resource-demanding alternative, establishing relationships between low-resolution GCM output and localized weather patterns \citep[\eg][]{schmidt2024wind .}
elements.sent-2411-027: However, these methods still require high-resolution data to establish such relationship.
elements.sent-2411-028: While RCMs and statistical downscaling techniques can add value in regions with complex terrain or significant land-sea contrasts, they do not always outperform raw GCM data \citep{rummukainen2016added .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Furthermore, for CMIP6 there is no unified collection of RCM data available.
elements.sent-2411-030: Given these data limitations, there is a need to investigate whether raw GCM data from CMIP6 can still be valuable for multi-decadal wind power forecasts–despite their lower spatial resolution.
elements.sent-2411-031: The value of climate models is difficult to assess as climate model outputs are not spatio-temporally aligned.
elements.sent-2411-032: Comparing climate model output to reanalyses such as ERA5 is, therefore, non-trivial.
elements.sent-2411-033: Typically, climate models are validated based on aggregate statistics using their historical runs \citep[e.g.][]{randall2007climate .}
elements.sent-2411-034: In the context of wind power evaluation, an additional challenge arises as the actual variable of interest--wind power--is a derived variable.
elements.sent-2411-035: The variable projected by climate models is wind speed and the relationship between power and speed is complex.
elements.sent-2411-036: To still account for the known non-linearities, other research considers wind speeds cubed \citep{miao2023evaluation , simulates load factors \citep{macleod2018transforming or transforms wind speeds to wind power using a turbine power curve \citep{ko2022projected .}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: In the following, we investigate historical wind speeds and derived wind power to analyze how the spatial resolution of global and regional climate model data affects multi-decadal wind power forecasts.
elements.sent-2411-038: In \Cref{methods we explain how we evaluate different wind (power) projections, in \Cref{results we describe our results and in \Cref{discussion we discuss our results as well as shortcomings and future work.}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: To compare the influence of climate model resolution on multi-decadal wind power forecasts, we investigate wind speeds using climate models and subsequent wind power predictions.
elements.sent-2411-040: In the following, we describe the data we use and how we perform evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-041: In our analysis, we compare the reanalysis dataset ERA5 \citep{hersbach2020era5 to global climate model data from ten CMIP6 models \citep{eyring2016overview as well as regional climate model data from CORDEX \citep{giorgi2015regional based on CMIP5 global boundary models \citep{taylor2012cmip5overview .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: An overview of all GCMs and RCMs used can be found in \Cref{tab:models_information and \Cref{tab:models_information_rcms , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: We use wind velocities $u$ and $v$ at six-hourly instantaneous resolution as these were found to be reliable for multi-decadal predictions in \citet{effenberger2023mind ) and compute wind speeds $w$ as \begin{equation}w = \sqrt{v^2+u^2}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Our study region covers all data points in continental Europe within a rectangle with longitudes $\in (25^{\circ , 73^{\circ )$ and latitudes $\in (-30^{\circ , 42^{\circ )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: This means we consider gridded data over land, as shown in \Cref{fig:grid-europe .}
elements.sent-2411-046: We focus on historical runs of climate models from 2005 to 2015.
elements.sent-2411-047: We choose the first ensemble member run typically labeled as r1i1p1f1.
elements.sent-2411-048: The original wind speeds $w_{10}$ are available at \SI{10 {\meter above ground\footnote{For the MPI CMIP5, the only available six-hourly wind speed data at the time of research, provided on the ESGF node \citep{MPI_CMIP5_historical , were at an altitude of \SI{1500 {\meter .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Therefore, $w_{10}$ changes to $w_{1500}$ in the power law to extrapolate the CMIP5 GCM model data to \SI{126 {\meter .. .}}
elements.sent-2411-050: To account for common wind turbine hub heights, we interpolate all wind speeds to \SI{126 {\meter using a wind profile power law following \begin{equation}w_{126}=\left(\frac{126}{10}\right)^\alpha \cdot w_{10},\end{equation} where the coefficient $\alpha$ is empirically derived to be about $\frac{1 {7}$ for neutral stability conditions \citep{peterson1978use .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Since the regional model runs from CMIP5 are on a curvilinear grid, we re-grid them to a regular Gaussian grid with a matching spatial resolution (\SI{0.1 {\degree ) using bilinear interpolation \citep{schulzweida_2023_10020800 .}}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/grid_plot.pdf} \caption{Example of gridded wind speed data over Europe. A single time frame of the same day (01.01.2005) of the lowest resolution CMIP6 data set NCC-LR (A), the highest resolution CMIP6 data set MOHC-HR (B) and the ERA5 reanalysis data set (C).} \label{fig:grid-europe}
elements.sent-2411-052: The evaluation is performed on two aspects: wind speed and wind power.
elements.sent-2411-053: While wind power is our ultimate variable of interest, the analysis primarily centers around wind speed, the variable provided by the climate models.
elements.sent-2411-054: Wind power can be estimated from wind speed using a theoretical wind power curve.
elements.sent-2411-055: Due to the non-linear nature of this relationship, however, a comprehensive analysis of wind speed distributions is essential for understanding its implications for wind power.
elements.sent-2411-056: Due to the non-linear relationship between wind speed and wind power ({compare \Cref{fig:power-curve ), models must capture the entire wind speed distribution and not only the mean quantities.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: To analyze these distributions, we investigate kernel density estimations (KDEs) and use quantitative methods to compare wind speed distributions.
elements.sent-2411-058: Since there is no universal metric for comparing distributions, we employ the Jensen-Shannon distance and the Wasserstein-1 distance, each providing a distinct approach to quantifying differences between distributions.
elements.sent-2411-059: We use the Jensen-Shannon (JS) distance to measure how closely a GCM's KDE wind speed distribution aligns with the ERA5 KDE wind speed distribution.
elements.sent-2411-060: This metric is a symmetrical and smooth variant of the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence \citep{endres2003new , and is defined as \begin{equation}\text{JS}(P_{\text{ref}}, P_{\text{pred}}) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2} \text{KL}(P_{\text{ref}} \parallel M) + \frac{1}{2} \text{KL}(P_{\text{pred}} \parallel M)} \text{\,,}\end{equation} where $M$ is the mixture distribution \begin{equation}\text{M} = \frac{1}{2}(P_{\text{ref}} + P_{\text{pred}}) \text{\,,}\end{equation} and $ \text{KL (P_{\text{ref \parallel P_{\text{pred )$ is the KL divergence between the reference distribution $P_{\text{ref }$ and the predicted distribution $P_{\text{pred }$, defined as \begin{equation}\text{KL}(P_{\text{ref}}\parallel P_{\text{pred}})=\sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} (P_{\text{ref}}(x) \log \left(\frac{P_{\text{ref}}(x)}{P_\text{{pred}}(x)}\right) \text{\,.}\end{equation} An advantage of the JS distance is that there are no issues related to undefined or infinite values when comparing distributions with zero wind speeds.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Additionally, the JS distance is bounded between $[0, 1]$ (unlike the Wasserstein-1 distance), offering an intuitive interpretation of the results as a similarity measure.
elements.sent-2411-062: As an additional way of comparing the wind speed distributions between the GCMs and ERA5 we use the Wasserstein-1 (W1) distance.
elements.sent-2411-063: The metric is based on the concept of optimal transport and differs from JS distance in interpretation and mathematical properties.
elements.sent-2411-064: The W1 distance is defined as \begin{equation}\mathrm{W}(P_{\text{pred}}, P_{\text{ref}}) = \inf_{\gamma \in \Pi(P_{\text{pred}}, P_{\text{ref}})} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \gamma} [ \| x - y \|_1 ] \text{\,,}\end{equation} where $\Pi(P_{\text{pred , P_{\text{ref )$ is the set of couplings, i.e., joint distributions whose marginals are $P_{\text{pred }$ and $P_{\text{ref }$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: Conceptually, the W1 distance quantifies the expected cost of the optimal transport plan that transforms the distribution $P_{\text{pred }$ into $P_{\text{ref }$ \citep{arjovsky2017wasserstein .}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Finally, we shift focus to the highest wind speeds resolved by the model.
elements.sent-2411-067: We expect these extreme values to be linked with spatial resolution, as higher-resolution models may better capture more complex atmospheric processes, potentially leading to a more accurate representation of extreme events.
elements.sent-2411-068: In this context, we consider the average of the 100 highest wind speeds to evaluate model performance.
elements.sent-2411-069: Percentile-based evaluation is avoided, as differences in model resolution would result in varying data points for the same percentile across models.
elements.sent-2411-070: The CMIP6 datasets differ in their latitude and longitude coordinates, and we use the number of spatial points within the selected area to measure the grid resolution.
elements.sent-2411-071: To examine trends in distance metrics and maximum wind speeds with increasing spatial resolutions, we perform linear-log regression \citep{montgomery2021introduction .}
elements.sent-2411-072: We can then describe the distance of each model to ERA5 as a function of the resolution $r$ \begin{equation}w_{max}(r) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\log(r) + \epsilon\end{equation} where $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N (0, \sigma^2)$ is Gaussian noise and $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ are computed using the method of least squares.}
elements.sent-2411-073: So far, we focused on wind speed, as wind directions are a direct output from GCMs.
elements.sent-2411-074: However, wind power is our variable of interest and its estimation involves assumptions and simplifications.
elements.sent-2411-075: While wind speed is a proxy, the ultimate goal is to understand how variations in wind speed distributions impact (multi-decadal) wind power estimation.
elements.sent-2411-076: To compute the wind power generation at each grid point, we input the wind speeds at grid points into a wind turbine power curve \citep{sabine_haas_2024_10685057 .}
elements.sent-2411-077: In the main part of the paper the chosen wind turbine power curve is the Vestas V126-3.45 turbine, with a hub-height of $126 \text{ m}$, see \Cref{fig:power-curve .}
elements.sent-2411-078: We provide supporting results for other turbines in the \Cref{appendix .}
elements.sent-2411-079: Finally, we compute the cumulative wind power generation by summing over the power generation of all time steps and grid points.
elements.sent-2411-080: The power predictions from the climate models are normalized by the ERA5 predictions to compare relative cumulative wind power estimates across different models.
elements.figure-2411-002: [H] \includegraphics[width=0.5\linewidth]{fig/power_curve_V126.pdf} \caption{Power curve of the Vestas V126-3.45 turbine. No power is generated for wind speeds below the cut-in wind speed $w_i$ and above the cut-out wind speed $w_o$. Between $w_i$ and the rated wind speed $w_r$ the relationship between speed and power is almost cubic. Between $w_r$ and $w_o$ the power output is maximal.} \label{fig:power-curve}
elements.sent-2411-081: We investigate the spatial resolution of GCMs and compare wind power predicted by different RCMs.
elements.sent-2411-082: Our results reveal that the choice of the underlying physical model- whether it is an RCM or GCM- is influential, and we do not find systematic trends with increasing resolution.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Higher resolution of GCM does not imply better forecast
elements.sent-2411-083: The results in \Cref{fig:wind-distributions reveal substantial differences in the estimated wind speed KDEs across models.}
elements.sent-2411-084: For instance, the JAP model notably deviates from the ERA5 wind speed distribution showing a distinct peak in the lower wind speed range.
elements.figure-2411-003: [H] \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/distribution_plot_europeland_126m_2005-2015.pdf} \caption{Kernel density estimations of the wind speeds (A) and of the corresponding wind power output (B). When datasets of the same model with different resolutions exist, the low-resolution version is indicated by a dashed line. Horizontal lines indicate the cut-in and cut-out wind speeds of the Vestas V126-3.45 turbine considered in this study. While most wind speeds are $< \SI{10}{\meter\per\second}$, most of the wind power is generated by wind speeds $\sim \SI{10}{\meter\per\second}$.} \label{fig:wind-distributions}
elements.sent-2411-085: We analyze the JS distance and the W1 distance of the GCMs and ERA5 to quantify these distributional differences in wind speeds, as shown in \Cref{fig:trends .}
elements.sent-2411-086: These metrics show substantial variability in each model's ability to capture the wind speed distribution accurately.
elements.sent-2411-087: However, regression analysis shows no statistically significant relationship between the two metrics considered and spatial resolution.
elements.sent-2411-088: This suggests that, although the accuracy of wind speed distributions varies largely between models, this variation is not systematically linked to spatial resolution.
elements.sent-2411-089: The regression between the average of the $100$ highest wind speeds and the logarithm of spatial resolution is significant at the $5 \%$ level.
elements.sent-2411-090: This suggests a positive relationship between spatial resolution and the model's ability to represent high wind speeds.
elements.sent-2411-091: When wind speeds are converted to wind power, accurately capturing specific wind speed quantiles becomes critical.
elements.sent-2411-092: Despite this, the results for cumulative wind power estimated for ten years, shown as a percentage relative to ERA5 power output in \Cref{fig:cumulative_cmip6 , reflect similar trends observed in the wind speed distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-093: There is considerable variation in the estimated cumulative wind power across different models, with eight models overestimating wind power output relative to ERA5, while two models underestimate it.
elements.sent-2411-094: Furthermore, relative cumulative power forecasts differ widely, ranging from $-42.56\%$ to $68.16\%$ when being compared to ERA5.
elements.sent-2411-095: For example, the GCM IPSL shows the closest alignment with ERA5.
elements.sent-2411-096: When considering the GCMs CMCC and MOHC, the forecasts fall within a range that could be useful for practical applications.
elements.sent-2411-097: This raises the question: given that GCMs can already provide valuable forecasts, is the added complexity and computational cost of regional climate models justified?.
elements.figure-2411-004: [H] \begin{subfigure}{\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/legend_europeland_126m_2005-2015_with_regression.pdf} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/trends_europeland_126m_2005-2015_with_regression.pdf} \end{subfigure} \caption{The JS distance (A) and the W1 distance (B) between the wind speeds from CMIP6 and ERA5 and the average over the $100$ highest wind speed values (C) are plotted against the spatial resolution (values are given in \Cref{tab:values_js_w1_max}). When datasets of the same model with different resolutions exist, the low-resolution version is indicated by a cross instead of a circle. The gray line represents the log-linear regression fits with standard errors. The regression in (A) and (B) is not statistically significant at a 5\% significance level, while the regression in (C) is (see \Cref{tab:regression_details}).} \label{fig:trends}
elements.subsection-2411-004: RCM choice significantly influences the forecast
elements.sent-2411-098: We further investigate regional model data from CMIP5 and present the corresponding results in \Cref{fig:cumulative_cmip5 .}
elements.sent-2411-099: For the global model run of the MPI CMIP5 model, we use data extrapolated from a different height (see \Cref{methods ).}
elements.sent-2411-100: The predictions from CMIP5 overestimate wind power most compared to ERA5.
elements.sent-2411-101: In contrast, all regional model runs and the CMIP6 model run are closer to ERA5.
elements.sent-2411-102: Comparing the spread of the predictions from the MPI model that is downscaled with several regional models (orange in \Cref{fig:cumulative_cmip5 ) to the prediction of several GCMs downscaled with the regional model SMHI (yellow in \Cref{fig:cumulative_cmip5 ) reveals that the spread of different regional climate models is bigger than that of one regional model with different boundary conditions.}}
elements.sent-2411-103: This is also reflected by the variance of the relative power predictions, which is 10.87\% for the regional models with the same boundary conditions and 0.26\% for the MPI model with different boundary conditions.
elements.figure-2411-005: [H] \begin{minipage}{0.49\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/cumulative_wp_2005-2015.pdf} \caption{Relative cumulative power of CMIP6 data sets. When datasets with different resolutions run by the same model exist, the low-resolution version is indicated with a dashed line. The choice of GCM has a substantial influence on the power prediction; most GCMs overestimate wind power relative to ERA5.} \label{fig:cumulative_cmip6} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.49\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/cumulative_wp_1995-2005.pdf} \caption{Relative cumulative power of CMIP5 regional and CMIP6 MPI from 1995 to 2005. The spread of the cumulative wind power predictions from multiple RCMs using the output of the MPI model as boundary conditions is larger than the spread using one RCM with different boundary conditions.} \label{fig:cumulative_cmip5} \end{minipage}
elements.sent-2411-104: We evaluate how GCM's spatial data resolution affects the reliability of simulated wind speeds for multi-decadal wind power forecasts.
elements.sent-2411-105: Specifically, we focus on ten raw ensemble members of different CMIP6 GCMs over the period $2005 - 2015$, comparing wind speed simulations and derived wind power estimates for the European region against ERA5 reanalysis data.
elements.sent-2411-106: Additionally, we investigate whether regional downscaling adds value by comparing the performance of several regional models from CMIP5.
elements.sent-2411-107: We employ various statistical modeling and verification techniques, including directly evaluating wind power to account for the non-linear relationship between wind speed and generated power.
elements.sent-2411-108: Contrary to the common assumption that a higher resolution provides more reliable wind speeds \citep{molina2022added, pryor2012influence , we find no clear trend in wind accuracy with increasing spatial resolution.}
elements.sent-2411-109: Instead, we observe that the accuracy of wind speed simulations varies substantially across different models.
elements.sent-2411-110: This suggests that the choice of model has a greater impact on wind speed accuracy than spatial resolution itself.
elements.sent-2411-111: Our analysis of regional climate models shows only a small spread in predictions when using different boundary conditions with the same RCM.
elements.sent-2411-112: A much larger spread is visible when comparing different RCMs, further emphasizing the importance of model choice.
elements.sent-2411-113: Therefore, selecting an appropriate climate model should be approached case-by-case, guided by a systematic procedure like the one we present in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-114: The accuracy of GCMs depends on their ability to capture key climate processes, forcings, and feedback mechanisms \citep{doblas2021linking .}
elements.sent-2411-115: Increasing spatial resolution alone does not guarantee an improved representation of these complex processes.
elements.sent-2411-116: This aligns with findings on effective resolution \citep{klaver2020effective , where increases in spatial resolution do not proportionally translate into enhanced representation of meteorological phenomena, as measured by global kinetic energy spectra.}
elements.sent-2411-117: Therefore, we advocate for using CMIP6 models for multi-decadal wind power forecasts.
elements.sent-2411-118: Although CMIP6 may have a coarser resolution, it offers notable advancements over its predecessor CMIP5 in simulating climate dynamics and providing more reliable multi-decadal forecasts \citep{miao2023evaluation .}
elements.sent-2411-119: In line with this rationale, we refrain from applying bias correction or downscaling in our analysis, recognizing that these techniques cannot correct fundamental model misrepresentations and may even disrupt the internal consistency of the climate models \citep{maraun2016bias, franccois2020multivariate .}
elements.sent-2411-120: Although our analysis shows no clear trend in wind speed accuracy with increasing spatial resolution, we do find that high-resolution models consistently perform better in capturing the upper tail of the wind speed distribution.
elements.sent-2411-121: This suggests that the benefits of (high-quality) high-resolution models are particularly critical in applications focused on extreme wind events, such as risk assessment.
elements.sent-2411-122: The IPSL model performs exceptionally well in simulating wind speeds and wind power over Europe, while the MPI-LR model performs the least well.
elements.sent-2411-123: However, our findings are likely region-specific and several limitations must be considered.
elements.sent-2411-124: We simplify our analysis by modeling wind power with a theoretical power curve and interpolating vertically to model wind speeds at a standard hub height.
elements.sent-2411-125: Although ERA5 reanalysis is widely recognized as a reasonable reference \citep{molina2021comparison, olauson2018era5, kaspar2020regional , it still contains notable biases that affect wind power simulations \citep{staffell2016using .}}
elements.sent-2411-126: Climate information is essential for multi-decadal wind power forecasting.
elements.sent-2411-127: Available climate models, among other things, vary substantially in their spatial resolution.
elements.sent-2411-128: We analyze 11 global and regional climate models for their ability to predict long-term wind power.
elements.sent-2411-129: Our findings highlight the need for high-quality GCMs and RCMs, particularly for wind energy planning.
elements.sent-2411-130: Given the sensitivity of wind power generation to local climate conditions, it is essential that models accurately capture both large-scale dynamics and more local meteorological phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-131: Higher-resolution climate models enhance the representation of extreme wind speeds, yet they do not guarantee improved accuracy in wind speed forecasts.
elements.sent-2411-132: Instead, the selection of the climate models, both GCM and RCM, emerges as a more influential factor for wind speed and power predictions than the spatial resolution itself.
elements.sent-2411-133: As we do not find substantial differences between CMIP5 regional models and CMIP6 global models, we recommend selecting climate models from CMIP6 for multi-decadal wind speed forecasts, as they offer the most current advancements in climate modelling.
elements.sent-2411-134: This research was funded by the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (DFG, German Research Foundation) under Germany’s Excellence Strategy – EXC number 2064/1 – Project number 390727645, the Tübingen AI Center, and the Athene Grant of the University of Tübingen.
elements.sent-2411-135: The authors thank the International Max Planck Research School for Intelligent Systems (IMPRS-IS) for supporting Nina Effenberger and Luca Schmidt.
